title: Ultra-Processed Food: What It Does to Your DNA and What They Don't Tell You
description: Recent studies show that ultra-processed food shortens telomeres, alters DNA methylation, and accelerates biological aging. We analyze the mechanisms with real data.

segments.voice: 58 percent of the calories consumed by Spaniards come from ultra-processed foods according to the 2023 ANIBES study. In the UK, it reaches 57 percent, and in the US, 67 percent. Now, the most recent studies are revealing that these foods not only lead to weight gain: they are literally altering the expression of our genes and accelerating biological aging.
segments.visual: ultra-processed food junk food packaging supermarket calories diet

segments.voice: What exactly is an ultra-processed food? The NOVA classification, developed by Carlos Monteiro at the University of Sao Paulo, defines it as foods that contain ingredients not used in domestic cooking and that have been industrially formulated with functional additives. This includes everything from breakfast cereals and industrial cold cuts to soft drinks, pastries, cookies, packaged sauces, and many flavored yogurts.
segments.visual: NOVA classification ultra-processed food ingredients industrial additives

segments.voice: Telomeres are the protective caps on the ends of chromosomes. With each cell division, they shorten slightly, and their length is one of the most reliable markers of biological aging. A 2020 study in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition with 886 adults from the Spanish PREDIMED-Plus cohort found that consuming three or more daily servings of ultra-processed foods was associated with a 29 percent higher probability of having short telomeres.
segments.visual: telomeres chromosomes aging biological age DNA caps short

segments.voice: Epigenetics is the second level of impact. A 2024 study in Nature Aging with 1,007 adult participants compared the Horvath epigenetic clock, measured by DNA methylation, with dietary patterns. Individuals with higher ultra-processed food consumption showed a biological age 3.8 years older than their chronological age compared to those who followed a predominantly non-processed diet. Each additional daily serving of ultra-processed food added 0.4 years of biological aging.
segments.visual: epigenetic clock methylation DNA aging ultra-processed biological age

segments.voice: What are the molecular mechanisms? First, chronic low-grade inflammation. Ultra-processed foods are low in fiber and high in free sugars, trans fats, and low-quality saturated fats. These components increase intestinal permeability and blood levels of bacterial endotoxins, a process called metabolic endotoxemia that chronically activates NF-kappaB and the production of IL-6 and TNF-alpha.
segments.visual: gut permeability endotoxemia inflammation metabolic barrier intestinal

segments.voice: Specific additives also have documented molecular effects. Emulsifiers like polysorbate 80 and carboxymethylcellulose, found in ice cream, sauces, and industrial bread, severely altered the microbiome of mice in a 2015 Georgia State study, inducing colitis and systemic inflammation at doses equivalent to human consumption. Follow-up human studies published in 2022 showed similar correlations.
segments.visual: emulsifiers polysorbate carboxymethylcellulose food additive gut microbiome

segments.voice: The nitrates and nitrites in processed meats are converted into carcinogenic nitrosamines in the stomach. The IARC of the WHO classified processed meats as a group 1 carcinogen, with sufficient evidence that they cause colorectal cancer. A 2021 analysis in the International Journal of Epidemiology with 475,000 European participants estimated that 4.4 percent of colorectal cancers would be attributable to processed meat consumption.
segments.visual: processed meat nitrites nitrosamines cancer colorectal risk WHO IARC

segments.voice: Excessive free sugar activates protein glycation. Glycosylated proteins, known as AGE (advanced glycation end-products), accumulate in tissues, harden arteries, damage skin collagen, and generate free radicals. A 2023 study in JAMA Network Open showed that for every additional 100 grams of daily free sugar, plasma AGE levels increased by 22 percent, and arterial stiffness measured by pulse wave velocity rose by 8 percent.
segments.visual: advanced glycation end products AGE sugar proteins artery stiffness

segments.voice: The excess salt in ultra-processed foods has a direct effect on the microbiome. Experiments by the University of Berlin in 2018 showed that a high-salt diet dramatically reduced Lactobacillus in the intestines of mice and humans. Lactobacillus produces lactic acid, which maintains intestinal pH and protects against hypertension. Human participants who took additional salt showed a reduction in their protective microbiome in just 2 weeks.
segments.visual: salt sodium microbiome lactobacillus gut hypertension blood pressure

segments.voice: A 2024 meta-analysis in The BMJ that analyzed 45 studies with 9.8 million participants established the most robust associations between ultra-processed foods and specific diseases. The highest risk was found for: premature death from any cause +21 percent, type 2 diabetes +40 percent, cardiovascular disease +22 percent, depression +22 percent, and thyroid cancer +58 percent.
segments.visual: ultra-processed food disease risk BMJ meta-analysis statistics 2024

segments.voice: The impact on the microbiome deserves its own chapter. A 2021 study in Cell with 21 participants who followed fermented food versus ultra-processed food diets for 10 weeks showed that ultra-processed foods reduced intestinal bacterial diversity by 14 percent and increased markers of systemic inflammation. The fermented food diet increased diversity by 19 percent and reduced inflammation.
segments.visual: microbiome diversity ultra-processed fermented food bacteria gut health

segments.voice: Ultra-processed foods specifically affect the brain through the gut-brain axis. A 2023 study in JAMA Psychiatry with 31,712 participants followed over 8 years found that consuming ultra-processed foods above the highest tertile was associated with a 23 percent higher risk of depression and a 28 percent higher risk of anxiety. The proposed mechanism includes alteration of the microbiome, reduction of neurotransmitters produced by intestinal bacteria such as 90 percent of the body's serotonin.
segments.visual: gut brain axis serotonin microbiome depression anxiety ultra-processed

segments.voice: What to do in practice? It's not about perfectionism but proportions. The 80-20 rule has a scientific basis: in the PREDIMED-Plus study, adhering to a Mediterranean diet in more than 75 percent of meals virtually eliminated the additional risk attributable to ultra-processed foods consumed in the remaining 25 percent. Cooking more, reading labels, and recognizing the ingredients of what you eat is the most accessible and impactful preventive habit.
segments.visual: Mediterranean diet home cooking reading labels healthy food choice
